coredata.dc:title: Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots with tailored surface functional groups for bioimaging of zebrafish, flocculation of heavy metal ions and ethanol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zonglin
Li, Baoqiang
Shi, Xinyao
Li, Li
Feng, Yujie
Jia, Dechang
Zhou, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Target-oriented synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots with high synthetic yield is still a challenge. Herein, the acrylamide (AM) and acrylic acid (AA) which containing unsaturated carbon–carbon double bonds (CC) and different functional groups were chosen as switches for the directional synthesis of carrot derived carbon dots with water-solubility (WCDs) and ethanol-solubility (ECDs). The synthetic yield of WCDs and ECDs could reach up to 31.19% and 51.94% by the directional hydrothermal carbonization reaction adjusted by the free radical polymerization. Specifically, the WCDs inherited negative charge, amino group and high N element from the AM, while AA endowed the ECDs with electronic neutrality, ester group and low N content. Furthermore, the WCDs as probes for assessment of uptake and metabolism of zebrafish, integrated of flocculation and detection of the WCDs on heavy metal ions, and the ethanol detection performance of the ECDs in medical alcohol disinfectant were detailly investigated. Therefore, the high synthetic yield CDs prepared by target-oriented synthesis have application potential in bioimaging, flocculation and detection of heavy metal ions, and ethanol detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Target-oriented synthesis
Free-radical polymerization
High synthetic yield
Carbon dots
Multiple applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of WCDs and ECDs derived from carrot
Characterization of optical and surface chemistry of WCDs and ECDs derived from carrot
Uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish
WCDs for integration of flocculation and detection
Target-oriented high-yield synthesis of WCDs and ECDs
Optical, phase and surface chemistry of WCDs and ECDs
Uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish
WCDs for integration of flocculation and detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Target-oriented and high-yield synthesis of WCDs and ECDs derived from carrots. (A) Unsaturated CC bond with different functional groups compounds targeted synthesis of WCDs and ECDs from carrot. Acrylamide (AM) and Acrylic acid (AA) guide conversion of carrots into WCDs and ECDs via monomer thermal polymerization in the condition of hydrothermal carbonization, respectively. (B) Photographs of WCDs solution and synthetic yield of WCDs derived from carrots guiding by AM and AA with different CC contents. Black precipitation appeared at the bottom of the bottle without AM or AA, however, it disappeared when AM and AA increased to more than 10%. The synthetic yield increases from 2.12% to 31.19% with elevated CC content. The highest synthetic yield could reach about 31.19% when AM was 20%; (C) Photographs and synthetic yield of WCDs derived from carrots and AA with different CC contents. The synthetic yield increases from 2.12% to 51.94% with elevated CC content from 0% to 15%. The highest synthetic yield was about 51.94% when AA was 15%.

UV–vis and fluorescent spectra of WCDs and ECDs. (A) UV–vis and excitation-dependent fluorescent spectra of WCDs, inset shows photographs of the WCDs solution under day light (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right). PL emission wavelength of WCDs shifts to longer wavelength (from 380 nm to 600 nm) as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 440 nm. The maximum emission is 450 nm when using 370 nm excitation; (B) Typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of WCDs. (C) UV–vis and excitation-dependent fluorescent spectra of ECDs, inset shows photographs of the ECDs solution under day light (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right). PL emission wavelength of WCDs shifts to longer wavelength (from 380 nm to 600 nm) as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 480 nm. The maximum emission is 500 nm when using 400 nm excitation; (D) Typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of ECDs.

(A) XRD pattern of the WCDs (Red line) and ECDs (Black line) exhibited a broad peak centered at 2θ = 20°; (B) FTIR spectrum of WCDs and ECDs. (C) XPS survey of the WCDs (i), XPS high resolution scans of the WCDs N1s (ii), C1s (iii) and O1s (iv); (D) XPS survey of the ECDs (i), XPS high resolution scans of the WCDs N1s (ii), C1s (iii) and O1s (iv). (E) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of WCDs and ECDs. (F) Cell viability of Hela cells after incubation with different concentrations of WCDs for 12 and 24 h by CCK8 assay.

Uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish embryos and larvae in E3 medium at WCDs of 500 μg mL−1. (A) Uptake and distribution of WCDs in zebrafish embryos, green fluorescence appeared in the yolk and embryonic membrane; (B) Uptake and distribution of WCDs in zebrafish larvae, green fluorescence were observed in the liver, yolk sac and intestine; (C) Fluorescence microscopy images of uptake of WCDs in zebrafish growth cycle; (D) Fluorescence microscopy images of uptake of WCDs in zebrafish larvae with different incubating time (WCDs = 500 μg mL−1); (E) Fluorescence microscopy images of metabolism of WCDs in zebrafish larvae with different incubating time.

WCDs severed as a fluorescent probe for flocculation and detection of heavy metal ions. (A) Schematic diagram of WCDs with a negatively charged surface for flocculation metal ions; (B) Photographs of copper ions flocculation by WCDs in different Cu2+ concentrations of 0 and 400 μM, WCDs can flocculate metal ions in high concentration condition; (C) Comparison of fluorescence intensities of WCDs (0.50 mg mL−1) different metal ions (500 μM); (D) Fluorescence quenching of WCDs in the presence of copper ions (0.0–300 μM) using 360 nm excitation; (E) The Stern–Volmer plot of WCDs at various concentrations of Cu2+ (0.0–250 μM) where F0/F are the PL intensities of WCDs (0.50 mg mL−1) in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively; (F) The dependence of (F0/F)-1 on concentrations of Cu2+ from 60 and 200 μΜ.

Naked-eye and fluorescent sensors for qualitative and quantitative ethanol detection, respectively. (A) Photographs of ECDs in ethanol–water solution with different concentrations of ethanol; (B) Fluorescence quenching of ECDs in the presence of ethanol (50%-100%) using 550 nm excitation; (C) The dependence of F0/F on concentrations of ethanol from 50% to 90%.
The quantum yield of WCDs and ECDs with different concentration of CC.

head.ce:title: Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots with tailored surface functional groups for bioimaging of zebrafish, flocculation of heavy metal ions and ethanol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Ceramics, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Ceramics, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Advanced Structural-Functional Integration Materials & Green Manufacturing Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Advanced Structural-Functional Integration Materials & Green Manufacturing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIIT Key Laboratory of Advanced Structural-Functional Integration Materials & Green Manufacturing Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science and Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: 92 West Street, P. O. Box 433, Harbin 100051, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Target-oriented synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots with high synthetic yield is still a challenge. Herein, the acrylamide (AM) and acrylic acid (AA) which containing unsaturated carbon–carbon double bonds (CC) and different functional groups were chosen as switches for the directional synthesis of carrot derived carbon dots with water-solubility (WCDs) and ethanol-solubility (ECDs). The synthetic yield of WCDs and ECDs could reach up to 31.19% and 51.94% by the directional hydrothermal carbonization reaction adjusted by the free radical polymerization. Specifically, the WCDs inherited negative charge, amino group and high N element from the AM, while AA endowed the ECDs with electronic neutrality, ester group and low N content. Furthermore, the WCDs as probes for assessment of uptake and metabolism of zebrafish, integrated of flocculation and detection of the WCDs on heavy metal ions, and the ethanol detection performance of the ECDs in medical alcohol disinfectant were detailly investigated. Therefore, the high synthetic yield CDs prepared by target-oriented synthesis have application potential in bioimaging, flocculation and detection of heavy metal ions, and ethanol detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a special quasi zero dimension material has widely used in biological imaging, photoelectric conversion and luminescent devices owing to their non-flickering fluorescence emission, multipotent absorption, good water-solubility and fine biological compatibility [1–6]. Among the various carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs, biomass energy is particularly attractive because of its multiplicity, renewability, abundance and low cost [7–10]. Currently, versatile biomass has been used for the synthesis of CDs, such as shaddock peel [11], banana juice [12], soy milk [13], egg white [14], and fibroin [15]. However, the synthetic yield of carbon dots is usually low when only biomass is used as a precursor because of the too many side reactions in the synthesis process and the reaction direction is uncontrollable. Although there is a report of high yield [16], the fact still cannot be negligible that the synthesis of CDs derived from biomass generally obtain WCDs with low yield (0.1% ~ 15%) [17–20]. Therefore, regulating the reaction direction and increasing the synthetic yield of carbon dots obtained from biomass is of great significance.

Additionally, carbon dots with different properties usually exhibit various applications in different fields. Fluorescence imaging has become an effective implies of visualization of living cells, with the advantages of no radiation risk, higher sensitivity, superior spatial determination, and fast flux. WCDs have good biocompatibility and photobleaching resistance so it has an extensive application prospect in the field of biological imaging [21–27]. With the help of fluorescence of WCDs and the explicit recognition of molecules with their structural characteristics or modifications, the detection of different ions or small molecules such as biomass sulfhydryl group, glucose and nucleotide can be realized [28–30]. ECDs are soluble in ethanol, methanol, acetone or isopentyl liquor, and slightly soluble in trichloromethane, but insoluble in water. ECDs have steady luminescence characteristics and can be used for sensitized solar cells and LED lighting [31]. Thus, target-oriented synthesis of WCDs and ECDs is urgent for promoting the development of carbon dots. However, few works focus on directional conversion of biomass to CDs with different solubility. On the other hand, both WCDs and ECDs can be obtained separately by using small molecule precursors and mixed solvents (ethanol water or phosphoric acid ethanol), but organic extractants are needed to separate the two, and the extractants usually have chemical toxicity. Liu et al. [32] synthesized WCDs and ECDs in an ethanol–water mixed solvent, while the extraction of chloroform was still needed to obtain WCDs and ECDs. Mao et al. [33] utilized WCDs and ECDs with micromolecule 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium and the synthetic yield ratio of WCDs to ECDs was adjusted by changing the molar ratio of phosphoric acid to ethanol in the reaction solvent. However, it is still necessary to use chloroform to extract and separate the reactants. Therefore, realizing the efficient directional conversion of a carbon source or the target-oriented synthesis in which WCDs and ECDs could be separately obtained without extraction and separation process is necessary.

Herein, in this article, unsaturated carbon–carbon double bonds (CC) compounds with different functional groups served as a switch to dominate the directional conversion of carrots into WCDs and ECDs with high synthetic yield was proposed and demonstrated. Obviously, both WCDs and ECDs with high yield can be synthesized directionally without further extraction and separation process by adding separate unsaturated double bond compounds. Specifically, WCDs exhibited great stability within the imaging of zebrafish in vivo uptake and metabolism assay, and good ability to flocculate metal ions at high concentration and detect the concentration of residual metal ions after flocculation at low concentration. While for ECDs, whether the content of ethanol products is high or low could be quickly distinguished by its ethanol solubility. And accurate detection of ethanol for high concentration ethanol products could also be accomplished. This work offers a promising strategy for commercialization and standardization of materials for large-scale applications and broadens the application of CDs.

Carrots were purchased from a local market and were beaten into juice in a juice extractor. Acrylamide (AM) and acrylic acid (AA) were recruited from Damao Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Ethanol, methyl alcohol, isoamyl alcohol, and tetrachloro methane were acquired from Tianjin Yaohua chemical reagent company. Deionized water (Millipore Milli-Q grade) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 was used. Dialysis bags (retained molecular weight 500 Da) were enrolled from Solarbio (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of WCDs and ECDs derived from carrot
ce:section.ce:para:
WCDs and ECDs derived from carrot were directedly synthesized by AM and AA via hydrothermal carbonization, respectively. Firstly, carrots and AM or AA with different CC contents (5, 10, 15, 20 wt %) were dissolved in water (20 mL). Secondly, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon with a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. Thirdly, the crude product was centrifuged with 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove large or agglomerated precipitates. The supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da against ultrapure water for 48 h, and then 0.22 μm filtered membrane was used for further removing redundant large particles. Finally, the filtrate was collected and lyophilized to get the final CDs powder product. The synthetic yield of the CDs was calculated based on our previous report [34], and the detailed synthetic yield of the CDs was determined according to the following equation:
S Y =
M
C D S
/
M P
× 100 %
where SY is the synthetic yield, MCDS and MP are the mass of the obtained CDs and the total quality of the precursor (carrots and AM or AA), separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of optical and surface chemistry of WCDs and ECDs derived from carrot
ce:section.ce:para:
The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was obtained with a Hitachi F-4600 fluorometer equipped with a Xe lamp under ambient conditions. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra was performed on TU 1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer. A time-resolved fluorescence decay curve was obtained on a Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer using a 405 nm laser as the excitation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was recorded using a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum One ranging from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Al/Ka as the source, and the energy step size was set at 0.100 eV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired on a Bruker Dimension Icon. The zeta potential measurement was determined by a Zeta-sizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK) at 25 °C. Taking quinine sulfate as the reference (excited at 360 nm), the excited light intensity at 360 nm and the absorbance at 360 nm were calculated, and the quantum yield (QY) was calculated. The samples were diluted to a certain concentration, the UV–vis absorption was below 0.05. The formula for calculating the QY of the CDs was as follows:
∅
C D s
=
∅
QS
×
I
CDs
I
QS
×
A
QS
A
CDs
×
n
C D s
n
Q S
2
× 100 %
where ϕ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area of the emission spectrum, A is the absorbance, and n is the refractive index. The subscripts of CDs and QS refer to CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.

Cytotoxicity was calculated using the CCK8 assay against Hela cells. HeLa cell suspension of 100 μL (5000 cells / mL) was added into 96 well plate, and Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle's medium containing 10% (V / V) fetal bovine serum, L-glutamine (2 mM) and penicillin (50 U mL−1) / streptomycin (50 μg mL−1) was added to each well, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in 5% CO2 humidification atmosphere. WCDs were injected into each well at the concentrations of 50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 mg / mL, and incubated for 12 h and 24 h, respectively. The medium was removed and the cells were washed with phosphate buffer (PBS). Then, 10 μL of CCK8 solution was added to each well. The optical density of the mixture at 450 nm was measured after further culture for 4 h. Cell viability was expressed as a percentage of absorbance relative to the control group, which was obtained without WCDs. Each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Type AB zebrafish (liberally given by the Institute of Hydrobiology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Wuhan) was kept beneath 14 h light/10 h dark cycles in 28 °C circulating freshwater aquaria (AAE-022-AA-A, Taiwan) and under the best culture conditions. The zebrafish were incubated in E3 culture medium (5 mM NaCl, 0.17 mM KCl, 0.33 mM CaCl2·2H2O, 0.33 mM MgSO4·7H2O) with WCDs (500 μg mL−1). The zebrafish was removed from the culture medium, washed three times and preserved in pure E3 medium for bioimaging after an incubation of 2 h. The optical imaging was performed on a microscope (Nikon Eclipse Ti S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: WCDs for integration of flocculation and detection
ce:section.ce:para: The WCDs powder was dissolved in deionized water to obtain a concentration of 0.50 mg mL−1. Then, an aqueous solution containing different kinds of metal ions with a concentration of 500 μM was prepared. The WCDs solution of 1 mL was mixed with 3 mL of the metal ion solution, and then the PL spectrum was obtained to evaluate the selectivity of WCDs. To measure the detection extent of Cu2+, 1 mL of the WCDs solution was blended with 3 mL of the solutions containing different concentrations of Cu2+, separately. All the samples were excited (λex) recorded at 360 nm for comparison.

The freeze-dried ECDs powder was dissolved in ethanol with a concentration of 50 mg mL−1. To evaluate the detection extent of ethanol, 1 mL of the ECDs solution was conflated with 5 mL of the aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of ethanol (20%-100%). After ultrasound for 30 min, centrifuge at high speed, and then supernatant liquor was collected. All the samples were excited (λex) at 550 nm and each sample were recorded for comparison.
Each test was performed leastways three times. All data were indicated as mean ± SD. The Student's T-test was used to appraise the statistical difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-oriented high-yield synthesis of WCDs and ECDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to directionally synthesize the WCDs and ECDs with high yield, different carbon–carbon double bonds in the process of hydrothermal carbonization were introduced, and WCDs and ECDs can be obtained by thermal polymerization of precursors (Fig. 1 A). In our previous study, it has been demonstrated that carbon–carbon double bonds may meliorate the synthetic yield of CDs, and polymerization is the key process for the synthesis of CDs [34]. Purification of WCDs and ECDs can be accomplished only by centrifugation and dialysis, and extraction with toxic organic solvents could be avoided. Different kinds of carbon–carbon double bonds precursors endow WCDs and ECDs with different organic element content, surface charge and structure, resulting in distinct solubility and optical properties. WCDs and ECDs can be dissolved in different solvents, and different kinds of blue light can be observed under UV excitation. (Fig. 1A).

To study for the influence of carbon–carbon double bonds compounds on the yield of WCDs and ECDs synthesis, acrylamide (AM) and acrylic acid (AA) as unsaturated precursors were employed, and the effects of different contents of carbon–carbon double bonds content on the synthetic yield were explored. The synthetic yield of WCDs increased by 14.7 times (from 2.12% to 31.19%) when the CC content increased from 0 to 20 wt%, and the sediments with significant CC content dependent characteristics decreased (Fig. 1B). The synthetic yield of ECDs was steadily increased by 24.5 times (from 2.12% to 51.94%) (Fig. 1C). Therefore, the content of carbon–carbon double bond compounds plays an important part in the synthetic yield of CDs in hydrothermal reactions. During the hydrothermal carbonization, the free radical initiator of AA or AM will be cracked into primary free radicals under high temperature, and then the AA or AM as the fluorescent carrier will combine with the primary free radicals to generate monomer free radicals. Afterwards, with the rise of the temperature, monomer free radicals will continuously undergo free radical polymerization with AA or AM. And the whole process is similar to the addition polymerization reaction which involves the opening of the carbon–carbon double bond. Therefore, the reaction is unidirectional, namely, the precursor undergoes a directional conversion, which avoids the problem of low conversion efficiency caused by the reverse reaction. In addition, during the hydrothermal carbonization process, the non-conjugated polymer chains in a cross-linked state facilitate to fix the converted intermediates and chelating functional precursors in a certain extent, which will further avoid the loss of raw materials. Thus, the addition of CC can regulate the reaction direction in the process of hydrothermal carbonization and ultimately improve the synthetic yield of carbon dots. All these results indicate that our work may afford a competent method for target-oriented synthesis of high yield WCDs and ECDs, and provide an essential basis for mass producing and target-oriented synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, phase and surface chemistry of WCDs and ECDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the WCDs and ECDs were characterized using UV–vis and PL. As clarified in Fig. 2 A, the absorption spectrum of the WCDs (from 20% AM + carrots) has two characteristic absorption peaks at 194 nm and 250–300 nm, which belong to the π- π* electronic transition of the aromatic system (the sp2 carbon network) and the distinctive absorption of surface n-π* carbonyl, separately. The well-dispersed suspension is transparent in sunlight and bright blue luminescence under UV excitation (inlet in Fig. 2A). When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 440 nm, the emission wavelength of PL changed to a shorter wavelength. When the 370 nm excitation wavelength was used, the strongest fluorescence emission could be observed at 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 2B, the double exponential functions base on the non-linear least-squares analysis is used to fit the attenuation trajectory of WCDs. The calculated average lifetime τ is 3.41 ns, which is appropriate for bioimaging applications [35]. As instanced in Fig. 2C, the absorption spectrum of ECDs has two peaks at 205 nm and 250–300 nm, which are also the absorption of the π-π* electronic transition of the distinctive aromatic π system and n-π* electronic transition of carbonyl groups. The fully dispersed aqueous solution of ECDs is transparent in sunlight and bright blue-white luminescence in ultraviolet light (inlet in Fig. 2C). With the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 480 nm, PL emission shifts to short wavelength. The maximum excitation wavelength is 400 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 500 nm. The results from AFM images have exhibited that WCDs and ECDs are an approximate sphere and with great dispersion. The height of WCDs is between 2 and 3 nm and ECDs are between 3 and 6 nm (Fig. S1). These results were the same as those reported in the literature, which reported that CDs with larger size show longer wavelength emission [36]. The calculated average lifetime τ of WCDs is 2.55 ns (Fig. 2D). UV–vis and PL of WCDs and ECDs with different carbon–carbon double bond content (5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) have been identified respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, the maximum excitation wavelength and a maximum emission wavelength of WCDs move towards the direction of short wavelength with the increase of AM content. The maximum excitation wavelength changes from 400 nm to 370 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength change from 500 nm to 450 nm. WCDs have shown an absorption peak of π-π* vibration belonging to sp2 of the aromatic system near 194 nm. Besides, with the increasing content of AA, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of ECDs are 400 nm and 500 nm, respectively. It can be realized from the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectrum that ECDs have also shown an absorption peak near 205 nm which belonging to the aromatic system sp2 π-π* vibration. Furthermore, the target-oriented synthesized of WCDs and ECDs both showed obvious excitation wavelength dependence and concentration dependence. With the decrease concentration of WCDs and ECDs (CC content: 20%), the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength both move towards the short wavelength, and the maximum excitation intensity increases initially and then decreases, presenting a parabolic change. The maximum excitation wavelength of WCDs decreases from 380 nm to 330 nm with the decreasing concentration from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 0.02 mg mL−1. The maximum excitation wavelength of ECDs decreases from 490 nm to 340 nm with the decreasing concentration from 5 mg mL−1 to 0.05 mg mL−1. The maximum excitation wavelength and concentration are piecewise linear (Fig. S3). In addition, WCDs show obvious pH dependence. With the increase of pH value, the photoluminescence intensity increases initially and then decreases, attributing to the fact that the preparation of WCDs was based on edge carbene structure triplet luminescence. Because of the environmental sensitivity of carbene structure, the fluorescence quenched after the carbene structure was protonated [37]. The saw tooth point of carbene structure was protonated under strongly acidic conditions when pH = 1–3, formed a reversible complex, produced precipitation and reduced the concentration. With the increase of pH, H+ concentration would decrease, which resulted to the decline of the quenching effect. Meanwhile, carboxyl ionization caused the recession of electron-withdrawing property, and fluorescence was enhanced when pH = 4–10. When the pH was 11–13, the concentration of OH– increased, the electron cloud density of the oxygen atom on the hydroxide was higher than that on the carboxylic acid which was easy to build intermolecular hydrogen bonds caused the attenuation of fluorescence. The electrons in carbene structure σ and π orbital stimulated by light and transitioned to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The carboxyl group took up the sawtooth point and produced nonradiative recombination to restrain the fluorescence. This explains why the fluorescence quantum efficiency of WCDs rich in carboxyl functional groups is lower than ECDs (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the biocompatibility of WCDs, the viability of Hela cells cultured with different concentrations of WCDs was evaluated. Cytotoxicity assay results indicate that the viability of Hela cells could remain above 95% at 12 and 24 h (Fig. 3F). This phenomenon shows that WCDs has good biocompatibility and further proves the potential biological application of WCDs.

To investigate the potential application in a biosystem, the uptake and metabolism assay of WCDs in zebrafish has been demonstrated by in vivo bioimaging (Fig. 4 ). It can be observed that there are floating specular fluorescence in the embryonic membrane, indicating that WCDs entered the embryonic membrane through the membrane hole, and WCDs mainly existed in yolk during the embryonic stage of zebrafish (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B showed that green fluorescence distributes in the intestine, stomach, liver and yolk sac. As shown in Fig. 4C, it was observed that fluorescence gradually increases with the time in 48 h. The WCDs exhibited good biocompatibility and had no significant adverse effects on zebrafish fertilized eggs, such as death, teratology, and its hatching rate. It could be seen that green/red fluorescence firstly enhanced and then attenuated, and the fluorescence area in zebrafish decreased gradually with the extension of culture time in the fresh E3 medium (Fig. 4C). To further investigate the uptake assay of WCDs, zebrafish uptake of WCDs in different periods had been explored. With the growth of time, the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence area of zebrafish increased progressively (Fig. 4D). WCDs mainly existed in the yolk during the embryonic development of zebrafish. The survival of young zebrafish mainly depended on the consumption of nutrients in the yolk five days before fertilization. With the development of zebrafish, the yolk gradually shrank, the fluorescence area decreased, while the fluorescence intensity increased. WCDs in the body were gradually expelled from the body along with physiological metabolism in the metabolic process. And the fluorescence intensity and area gradually decreased because of the existence of WCDs in the remaining yolk and intestines. As shown in Fig. 4E, green/red fluorescence decreased with the increase of culture time, and the area gradually decreased when cultured in fresh E3 medium for 28 h. These results illustrate that WCDs are biocompatible and are appropriate candidates for bioimaging and in vivo tracking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WCDs for integration of flocculation and detection
ce:section.ce:para: As was well known, metal ions could easily bind to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of WCDs, which would lead to the polymerization of WCDs and the quenching of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the WCDs can be used for flocculation of heavy metal ions owing to the moieties of polyacrylamide in the precursor [38,39]. Taking copper as an example (Fig. 5B), when the concentration of Cu2+ is higher than 200 μM, significant precipitation occurred, and the color of the WCDs solution became pale. In addition, as revealed in Fig. 5C, multiple metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) induced effective decrease in PL intensity of WCDs. When the concentration of metal ions is more than 200 μM, the prepared carbon dots exhibited strong flocculation ability for removing multiple metal ions from the solution.

When the concentration of metal ions is less than 200 μM, the flocculation stops and WCD can be used to detect the contents of metal ions. And there was no obvious difference between different metal ions. To explore the sensitivity of WCDs for ions detection fluorescence probe, changes in PL intensity were explored in the existence of Cu2+. Obviously, WCDs solution shows intense fluorescence at 360 nm in the absence of Cu2+ while the existence of Cu2+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity, indicating that Cu2+ was able to quench fluorescence and CDs were beneficial to quick detection of Cu2+ [40]. Then PL intensity was explored under the condition of different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 300 μM. It can be seen from Fig. 5D that the PL intensity of WCDs decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, indicating that the sensor is sensitive to Cu2+. A parabolic relationship (0–250 μM) and a linear relationship (60–200 μM) exists between PL intensity and Cu2+ concentration. At a concentration of 0–250 μM, it fits parabolic relationship, the parabolic equation is (F0/F)-1 = 0.0000077X2 + 0.000498X-0.00505 (R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 5E). At a concentration of 60–200 μM, it accords the linear Stern-Volmer equation and elucidates the static quenching of Cu2+ and WCDs. The equation is (F0/F)-1 = 0.00248X-0.11232 (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 5F). At signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, the calculated detection limit for Cu2+ is 0.187 μM, which demonstrates that WCDs can be used as a promising fluorescent probe for the analysis of Cu2+ in biological and pharmaceutical samples. The fluorescence quenching of WCDs may be due to the multi-site coordination between Cu2+ and the oxygen or nitrogen-containing functional groups of WCDs, which destroys stability of the functional groups in WCDs and causes the aggregation and fluorescence quenching of WCDs.

Therefore, WCDs are possible to flocculate metal ions at high concentration and detect the concentration of residual metal ions after flocculation at low concentration. These results prove that WCD can be used as a flocculant with ion detection function, which is different from the copper ion probe reported in the past.

ECDs can be employed as an extremely competent probe for ethanol detection in high concentration alcohol products since ECDs exert good dispersion in ethanol. ECDs can be completely soluble in ethanol and methanol, rarely precipitates in isoamyl alcohol, precipitates a lot in formaldehyde, slightly soluble in chloroform and insoluble in tetrachloromethane (Fig. S8). In a word, ECDs exhibited satisfactory solubility and dispersion in low molecular alcohol, partly dissolved in formaldehyde and chloroform, and were insoluble in non-polar solvents such as tetrachloromethane, which indicated high surface polarity of ECDs.

To explore the capability of detecting the content of ethanol in the ethanol–water mixture, variations in PL intensity were analyzed in the existence of diverse content of ethanol. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the mixture solution with an ECDs concentration of 10 mg mL−1 can remain homogeneous when the ethanol content is above 50%. While with the decrease of ethanol content, ECDs gradually precipitate from the mixture solution when the ethanol content is less than 50%. Therefore, with the concentration of the mixture solution decreasing, the color of the solution will become pale. As revealed in Fig. 6B, the mixture solution exhibited intense fluorescence at 550 nm in an ethanol concentration of 100%. With the decrease of ethanol content, the PL intensity of the mixture reduces gradually when the ethanol content is above 50%. A good linear relationship exists between PL intensity and ethanol concentration. The equation is F0/F = 2.944–0.0181X (R2 = 0.999) (Fig. 6C). These results facilitated us to appraise the possibility of ECDs as an updated fluorescent detector for the content of ethanol in alcohol products. For ECDs, the fluorescence intensity of ECDs solution increased with the increase of ethanol concentration in a certain range. Therefore, the synthesized ECDs for the detection of high concentration ethanol was proposed and conducted. And for ethanol with high or low concentration, it can be easily identified by the naked eyes with the different solubility of ECDs. The combination of these two methods can detect the content of ethanol quickly and easily, and this method be used to identify the authenticity of alcoholic products.

High-yield of WCDs and ECDs (31.19% and 51.94% respectively) was synthesized by target-oriented route. By adjusting the concentration of unsaturated double bond compounds, the element content, surface state, and structure of CDs can be changed for regulating their optical properties. The potential use of the WCDs in in vitro imaging was proved by uptake and metabolism in zebrafish. Moreover, negative charged WCDs can be used as a flocculant with ion detection function, which was expected to be used in sewage treatment. Furthermore, the ECDs existed great dispersion in ethanol and was utilized to detect the content of ethanol rapidly and readily, which can be further used to identify the authenticity of alcohol products. Combining their simple and target-oriented synthetic method, optical properties, good biocompatibility, and sensitive detection, target-oriented synthesized CDs reveal feasible applications in bio-labeling and fluorescent sensors.

Zonglin Liu: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Validation. Baoqiang Li: Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xinyao Shi: Visualization, Data curation. Li Li: Bioiamging. Yujie Feng: Validation. Dechang Jia: Supervision. Yu Zhou: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972086 and 51621091), State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment of Harbin Institute of Technology (2019TS01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148118.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
